question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user flood the /api/v1/contracts/results/{transactionIdOrHash}/opcodes endpoint with concurrent GET requests (each triggering EVM re-execution via processOpcodeCall()) to exhaust all available CPU threads on the mirror node, causing the node to become unresponsive and unable to process or relay new transaction confirmations to the network?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: throttleManager.throttleOpcodeRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user bypass the throttleOpcodeRequest() gate by distributing requests across many source IPs (e.g., botnet or open proxies), such that per-IP throttle limits are never triggered individually but collectively saturate the EVM re-execution thread pool, causing total network transaction confirmation failure?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with stack=true, memory=true, and storage=true simultaneously for a known computationally expensive historical transaction, causing processOpcodeCall() to consume unbounded heap memory during EVM re-execution and trigger an OutOfMemoryError that crashes the JVM process, resulting in total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request with memory=true for a transaction that executed a contract with a very large memory footprint, causing the OpcodeService.processOpcodeCall() to allocate gigabytes of heap during replay, exhausting JVM memory and preventing the mirror node from processing any new transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: validateAcceptEncodingHeader()] Can an unprivileged external user bypass the gzip enforcement in validateAcceptEncodingHeader() by sending a header value like 'gzip, identity' or 'GZIP' (case variations handled by toLowerCase()) to pass validation but then receive an uncompressed response, causing the server to generate and buffer a massive uncompressed opcode trace that exhausts network bandwidth and server memory, leading to network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user submit thousands of concurrent requests each referencing a different valid transaction hash, causing the EVM to simultaneously re-execute thousands of transactions, exhausting the database connection pool used by OpcodeService and starving the mirror node's normal transaction ingestion pipeline of DB connections, causing a total network transaction confirmation halt?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: throttleManager.throttleOpcodeRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a race condition in throttleOpcodeRequest() where the throttle counter is read and incremented non-atomically, allowing many concurrent requests to slip through the throttle gate simultaneously and trigger a burst of EVM re-executions that overwhelms the node's processing capacity and halts new transaction confirmations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with a valid transactionIdOrHash pointing to a transaction that contains an infinite-loop or extremely deep recursive contract call, causing processOpcodeCall() to run indefinitely and hold a thread permanently, and by repeating this for all available threads cause a complete thread pool starvation that prevents any new transaction from being confirmed?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a request timeout on the getContractOpcodes() handler to hold HTTP worker threads open indefinitely by targeting transactions with extremely long EVM replay times, exhausting the Tomcat/Netty thread pool and preventing the server from accepting any new connections for transaction processing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: OpcodeRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user construct an OpcodeRequest with all boolean flags (stack=true, memory=true, storage=true) set to true for a transaction with a large storage trie, causing processOpcodeCall() to load the entire contract storage into memory for every opcode step, producing a response so large that serialization exhausts heap and causes JVM crash, shutting down the network?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly request opcodes for the same computationally expensive transaction hash without any per-transaction caching in OpcodeService, causing the EVM to re-execute the same expensive transaction repeatedly and monopolize CPU resources, preventing the mirror node from confirming new transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: validateAcceptEncodingHeader()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with Accept-Encoding header set to a very long string (e.g., 10MB of random characters containing 'gzip') to pass the validateAcceptEncodingHeader() check, causing excessive string processing and memory allocation in the header parsing layer, contributing to resource exhaustion that halts transaction confirmation?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: TransactionIdOrHashParameter.valueOf()] Can an unprivileged external user send a path variable with an extremely long string as transactionIdOrHash, causing TransactionIdOrHashParameter.valueOf() to perform expensive regex or string matching operations that consume CPU in a tight loop, and by flooding with such requests cause CPU exhaustion that prevents new transaction confirmations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that properties.isEnabled() is checked at runtime (not compile-time) and if the configuration is dynamically reloaded to enabled=true, immediately flood the endpoint before throttle limits are applied, causing a burst of EVM re-executions that crashes the node and halts network transaction confirmations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: throttleManager.throttleOpcodeRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a missing or weak throttle implementation in throttleOpcodeRequest() that only tracks request count but not resource consumption (e.g., gas used), allowing them to submit many low-gas requests that individually pass throttle but collectively saturate the EVM execution engine and prevent new transaction confirmations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user send concurrent requests with storage=true for transactions that touched contracts with extremely large storage tries, causing OpcodeService to load and serialize massive storage snapshots for each opcode step, exhausting heap memory and causing GC pressure that freezes the JVM and halts all transaction processing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of rate limiting per transaction hash in getContractOpcodes() to repeatedly replay the same gas-intensive transaction, causing the EVM re-execution engine to be permanently busy and unable to serve the mirror node's internal transaction confirmation pipeline?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request where transactionIdOrHash resolves to a transaction that triggers a deeply nested call stack during EVM replay, causing a StackOverflowError in the JVM during processOpcodeCall() that propagates uncaught and crashes the application thread, and by repeating this exhaust all threads and halt network transaction confirmations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: validateAcceptEncodingHeader()] Can an unprivileged external user send a null or missing Accept-Encoding header to trigger the HttpClientErrorException path in validateAcceptEncodingHeader(), and by flooding with such requests cause excessive exception object creation and GC pressure that degrades JVM performance to the point where new transaction confirmations are halted?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the synchronous blocking nature of processOpcodeCall() (EVM re-execution is CPU-bound) to hold HTTP request threads for extended periods, and by sending many such requests simultaneously exhaust the web server's thread pool, causing the mirror node to stop accepting new connections and halting transaction confirmation?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with stack=true for a transaction that executed thousands of opcode steps, causing processOpcodeCall() to build an enormous stack trace list in memory, and by sending many such requests simultaneously exhaust heap memory and trigger OOM that crashes the mirror node and halts network transaction confirmations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: throttleManager.throttleOpcodeRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a shared global throttle state in throttleOpcodeRequest() that is not partitioned by client IP, allowing a single attacker to consume the entire global throttle budget and deny service to all other users, effectively halting the network's ability to process transaction confirmations through the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a maximum response size limit in getContractOpcodes() to trigger generation of a response with millions of opcode entries (stack+memory+storage all true for a long-running transaction), causing the JSON serializer to allocate gigabytes of memory and crash the JVM, halting all transaction confirmations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: OpcodeRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user craft an OpcodeRequest where the transactionIdOrHashParameter refers to a contract deployment transaction with a very large bytecode, causing the EVM replay to spend excessive time decoding and executing the init code, monopolizing CPU resources and preventing the mirror node from confirming new transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/controller/OpcodesController.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: getContractOpcodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the HTTP keep-alive connection behavior to hold open many persistent connections to the opcodes endpoint, each with a slow-drip request body or slow header delivery (Slowloris attack), exhausting the server's connection pool and preventing new transaction-related requests from being processed?
